title: The Western Canon: The Books and School of the Ages
excerpt: Harold Bloom's provocative and erudite defense of the Western literary canon, arguing for the enduring aesthetic value of 26 essential writers from Shakespeare to Beckett.
keyIdeas: The Western canon is a tradition of literary works marked by their 'strangeness' and aesthetic originality
Canonical works achieve their status through 'anxiety of influence' — creative misreading of predecessors
The canon should be judged by aesthetic criteria, not political or social utility
keyTakeaways: Shakespeare is the center of the Western canon, the author against whom all others are measured
Canonical works are characterized by their originality, complexity, and ability to reward rereading
The canon is constantly evolving as new works enter and older works are revalued
Bloom identifies 26 'canonical' authors from Dante to Beckett as central to the Western tradition
The academic trend toward multiculturalism and historicism threatens the canon's survival
whoShouldRead: Serious readers of literature who care about the Western tradition
Students of literary criticism and theory
Educators thinking about what works to teach
Anyone who has ever wondered what makes a book 'great'
whoShouldSkip: Readers who reject the very idea of a literary canon
Those seeking diverse or multicultural literary perspectives
People who dislike Bloom's combative, opinionated style
